content: Which of the following molecules does not obey octet rule?
explanation: Chlorine is an example of an atom that can have more than 8 electrons in its valence shell (since it is in the third period and can expand its valence shell due to the presence of 3 d orbital). In $\mathrm{ClF}_3$, chlorine has 10 electrons in its valence shell. Therefore, $\mathrm{ClF}_3$ does not obey the octet rule.

content: Which of the following xenon compound has chlorine pentafluoride like structure?
explanation: $\mathrm{XeOF}_4$ and $\mathrm{ClF}_5$ both have square pyramidal molecular structures.

content: Which of the following is correct decreasing order of bond length regarding $\mathrm{N}_2, \mathrm{O}_2$ and $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ ?
explanation:
As bond order increases, bond enthalpy increases and bond length decreases.

$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline \text { Molecule } & \text { Structure } & \text { Bond order } \\ \hline \mathrm{Cl}_2 & \mathrm{Cl}-\mathrm{Cl} & 1 \\ \hline \mathrm{O}_2 & \mathrm{O}=\mathrm{O} & 2 \\ \hline \mathrm{~N}_2 & \mathrm{~N} \equiv \mathrm{~N} & 3 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$$ \therefore \quad \text { Decreasing order of bond length is } \mathrm{Cl}_2>\mathrm{O}_2>\mathrm{N}_2 \text {. } $$

content: Which of the following is more polar?
explanation: Since the resultant dipole moment of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ is higher, it is more polar.

content: Which of the following pair of compounds consists equal number of lone pair of electrons in the valence shell of central atom?
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Number of lone pairs }\\ &=\frac{\text { No. of valence } \mathrm{e}^{-}-\text {no. of bonding } \mathrm{e}^{-}}{2} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text { Compound } & \begin{array}{c} \text { No. of } \\ \text { Lone pairs } \end{array} & \text { Compound } & \begin{array}{c} \text { No. of } \\ \text { Lone pairs } \end{array} \\ \hline \mathrm{BrF}_5 & 1 & \mathrm{XeF}_6 & 1 \\ \hline \mathrm{ICl} & 3 & \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S} & 2 \\ \hline \mathrm{ClF}_3 & 2 & \mathrm{XeF}_2 & 3 \\ \hline \mathrm{IF}_7 & 0 & \mathrm{XeF}_4 & 2 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{BrF}_5$ and $\mathrm{XeF}_6$ have one lone pair of electrons in the valence shell of central atom.

content: What is the number of electrons in bonding molecular orbitals and antibonding molecular orbitals respectively in $\mathrm{F}_2$ molecule?
explanation:
$\mathrm{F}_2:(\sigma 1 \mathrm{~s})^2\left(\sigma^* 1 \mathrm{~s}\right)^2(\sigma 2 \mathrm{~s})^2\left(\sigma^* 2 \mathrm{~s}\right)^2\left(\sigma 2 \mathrm{p}_z\right)^2\left(\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_x\right)^2 \left(\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_y\right)^2\left(\pi^* 2 \mathrm{p}_x\right)^2\left(\pi^* 2 \mathrm{p}_y\right)^2$

No. of electrons in bonding orbitals $=10$

No. of electrons in antibonding orbitals $=8$.

content: What type of overlap is involved in the formation of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bonds in acetylene molecules?
explanation:
Step 1: Hybridization of carbon in acetylene

In acetylene, the structure is $\ce{H–C≡C–H}$.

Each carbon forms:

  • One $\ce{C–H}$ sigma bond

  • One $\ce{C–C}$ sigma bond

  • Two $\pi$ bonds (from overlap of unhybridized $p$ orbitals).

Thus, each carbon is $\mathrm{sp}$ hybridized (because it forms two sigma bonds with linear geometry).

Step 2: Overlap in the C–H bond

  • Each hydrogen has a 1s orbital.

  • On carbon, the orbital used for sigma bonding is an $\mathrm{sp}$ hybrid orbital.

Therefore, the C–H sigma bond arises from sp–s overlap.

✔ Correct Answer: Option C: $\mathrm{sp}-\mathrm{s}$

content: What is shape of interhalogen compound so that central halogen exhibits +3 oxidation state?

explanation:
The electronic configuration of $F_2$ molecule is${ }^{} (\sigma 1 \mathrm{~s})^2\left(\sigma^* 1 \mathrm{~s}\right)^2(\sigma 2 \mathrm{~s})^2\left(\sigma^* 2 \mathrm{~s}\right)^2\left(\sigma 2 \mathrm{p}_2\right)^2 \quad(\pi 2 \mathrm{p})^2 \left(\pi 2 p_{\mathrm{y}}\right)^2\left(\pi^* 2 p_{\mathrm{x}}\right)^2\left(\pi^* 2 p_{\mathrm{y}}\right)^2$

$\therefore \quad$ No. of electrons in antibonding orbitals $=8$

content: Identify the correct statement regarding geometry and lone pair of electrons present in $\mathrm{CH}_4$ and $\mathrm{SiCl}_4$.

options.content: Both have same geometry with two lone pair of electrons each.
Both have different geometry with one lone pair of electrons each.
Both have same geometry with no lone pair of electrons each.
Both have different geometry with no lone pair of electrons each.

explanation:
We are asked about the geometry and lone pairs in CH$_4$ and SiCl$_4$.

Step 1: Central atom in CH$_4$

  • Carbon has 4 valence electrons.

  • Forms 4 sigma bonds with 4 H atoms.

  • No lone pairs on the central atom.

  • Geometry: tetrahedral.

Step 2: Central atom in SiCl$_4$

  • Silicon has 4 valence electrons.

  • Forms 4 sigma bonds with 4 Cl atoms.

  • No lone pairs on the central atom.

  • Geometry: tetrahedral.

Conclusion:

Both CH$_4$ and SiCl$_4$ have same geometry (tetrahedral) and no lone pairs on the central atom.

✅ Correct answer:

Option C: Both have same geometry with no lone pair of electrons each.

explanation:

Therefore, 1 -single bond and 3 -double bonds are formed by chlorine with oxygen in perchloric acid.

content: Which of the following species is not tetrahedral?
explanation: $\mathrm{SF}_4$ has a trigonal bipyramidal electron geometry, and seesaw shaped molecular geometry.

content: Which of the following is Lewis base?
explanation:
A Lewis base is a substance that can donate a pair of electrons.

Let's check each option:

  • Option A: $\mathrm{BF}_3$

    $\mathrm{BF}_3$ is electron-deficient and accepts electron pairs. It is a Lewis acid.

  • Option B: $\mathrm{AlCl}_3$

    $\mathrm{AlCl}_3$ is also electron-deficient and acts as a Lewis acid.

  • Option C: $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$

    $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ is a cation that accepts electron pairs to form complexes. It is a Lewis acid.

  • Option D: $\mathrm{NH}_3$

    $\mathrm{NH}_3$ has a lone pair of electrons on nitrogen and can donate this pair to other species.

    Hence, it is a Lewis base.

Final Answer:

The correct answer is:

$ \boxed{\mathrm{NH}_3} $

content: Which of the following molecules consist minimum lone pair of electrons in the valence shell of central atom?

content: Which of the following molecules has a regular geometry as expected?
explanation:
Let us analyze each molecule using the VSEPR theory (Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion theory) as described in the NCERT textbook.

Option A: $\mathrm{SiCl}_4$

  • Central atom: Si

  • Valence electrons on Si: 4

  • Chlorine atoms: 4, each forms a single bond

  • Total bonding pairs: 4

  • Lone pairs on Si: 0

According to VSEPR theory:

  • Steric number $= 4$ (4 bond pairs + 0 lone pairs)

  • Geometry: Tetrahedral (regular geometry)

Option B: $\mathrm{SF}_4$

  • Central atom: S

  • Valence electrons on S: 6

  • Fluorine atoms: 4, each forms a single bond

  • Total bonding pairs: 4

  • Lone pairs on S: $6 - 4 = 2$ left as non-bonded electrons, which is $1$ lone pair

According to VSEPR theory:

  • Steric number $= 5$ (4 bond pairs + 1 lone pair)

  • Geometry: See-saw (not regular)

Option C: $\mathrm{BrF}_5$

  • Central atom: Br

  • Valence electrons on Br: 7

  • Fluorine atoms: 5, each forms a single bond

  • Total bonding pairs: 5

  • Lone pairs on Br: $7 - 5 = 2$ left as non-bonded electrons, which is $1$ lone pair

According to VSEPR theory:

  • Steric number $= 6$ (5 bond pairs + 1 lone pair)

  • Geometry: Square pyramidal (not regular)

Option D: $\mathrm{XeF}_4$

  • Central atom: Xe

  • Valence electrons on Xe: 8

  • Fluorine atoms: 4, each forms a single bond

  • Total bonding pairs: 4

  • Lone pairs on Xe: $8 - 4 = 4$ left as non-bonded electrons, which is $2$ lone pairs

According to VSEPR theory:

  • Steric number $= 6$ (4 bond pairs + 2 lone pairs)

  • Geometry: Square planar (all bond pairs are in the same plane and lone pairs are on opposite sides, so the geometry for the atom positions is regular)

But by the definition of "regular geometry" (all positions equivalent and bond angles the same), the square planar geometry, despite being symmetric, is not perfectly regular like tetrahedral because of lone pairs.

Final Answer

The molecule with a regular geometry as expected is:

$ \boxed{\mathrm{SiCl}_4} $

This has a tetrahedral (regular) geometry.

content: Which of the following compounds has maximum covalent character?
explanation:
To find which compound has maximum covalent character, we use Fajans' Rules (Class 11, NCERT, Chemical Bonding and Molecular Structure).

According to Fajans' Rules:

  • Smaller cation and larger anion → more covalent character.

  • Cations with higher charge polarise anions more.

  • Larger anion is more easily polarised.

Comparing the options:

  • Cation: Li$^+$ (smaller) vs Na$^+$ (larger)

  • Anion: Cl$^-$ (smaller) vs I$^-$ (larger)

Let's arrange as per covalent character:

  • Covalent character increases with smaller cation and larger anion.

  • Li$^+$ is smaller than Na$^+$, so Li-compounds will be more covalent than Na-compounds.

  • I$^-$ is larger than Cl$^-$, so I-compounds will be more covalent than Cl-compounds.

Among the given choices:

  • LiI: Smallest cation (Li$^+$), largest anion (I$^-$)

  • LiCl: Smallest cation (Li$^+$), smaller anion (Cl$^-$)

  • NaI: Larger cation (Na$^+$), largest anion (I$^-$)

  • NaCl: Larger cation (Na$^+$), smaller anion (Cl$^-$)

Hence, maximum covalent character will be in LiI.

Final answer:

Option B: LiI has the maximum covalent character.

content: Which of the following molecule can form hydrogen bonding with itself?
explanation: One molecule of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ associates with another molecule of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ by inter-molecular hydrogen bonding.

content: What is the number of electrons present in antibonding orbitals of $\mathrm{N}_2$ molecule according to molecular orbital theory?
explanation:
According to molecular orbital theory, the $\mathrm{N}_2$ molecule is made up of nitrogen atoms with each contributing 7 electrons, making a total of 14 electrons for the $\mathrm{N}_2$ molecule. These electrons are filled into molecular orbitals in the order of increasing energy.

The molecular orbital configuration for $\mathrm{N}_2$ is :

$$ (\sigma_{1s})^2 (\sigma^*_{1s})^2 (\sigma_{2s})^2 (\sigma^*_{2s})^2 (\pi_{2p_x})^2 (\pi_{2p_y})^2 (\sigma_{2p_z})^2 $$

In this configuration :

Bonding orbitals are: $\sigma_{1s}$, $\sigma_{2s}$, $\pi_{2p_x}$, $\pi_{2p_y}$, and $\sigma_{2p_z}$.

Antibonding orbitals are marked with an asterisk (*), specifically: $\sigma^*_{1s}$, and $\sigma^*_{2s}$.

Counting the electrons in the antibonding orbitals :

$\sigma^*_{1s}$ has 2 electrons.

$\sigma^*_{2s}$ has 2 electrons.

Thus, there are a total of 4 electrons in the antibonding orbitals.

So the number of electrons present in antibonding orbitals of the $\mathrm{N}_2$ molecule is 4.

Option C (04) is the correct answer.

content: Identify the angle $\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{S}-\mathrm{O}$ in $\mathrm{SO}_2$ molecule.
What is the total number of electrons present in bonding orbitals of $\mathrm{O}_2$ molecule according to molecular orbital theory?
explanation:
In the molecular orbital theory, the electronic configuration for the $\mathrm{O}_2$ molecule can be written as:

$$ \sigma_{1s}^2 \sigma_{1s}^{*2} \sigma_{2s}^2 \sigma_{2s}^{*2} \sigma_{2p_z}^2 \pi_{2p_x}^2 \pi_{2p_y}^2 \pi_{2p_x}^{*1} \pi_{2p_y}^{*1} $$

Bonding orbitals include all the orbitals without an asterisk () in their notation, while antibonding orbitals include those with an asterisk ().

For $\mathrm{O}_2$, the bonding orbitals and their respective electrons are:

$\sigma_{1s}^2$: 2 electrons

$\sigma_{2s}^2$: 2 electrons

$\sigma_{2p_z}^2$: 2 electrons

$\pi_{2p_x}^2$: 2 electrons

$\pi_{2p_y}^2$: 2 electrons

Adding the electrons in these bonding orbitals gives a total:

$$ 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 10 $$

Thus, the total number of electrons present in the bonding orbitals of the $\mathrm{O}_2$ molecule is 10. Hence, option C is correct.

content: Which of the following molecule has bond order 2 ?
explanation:
The electronic configuration of $\mathrm{O}_2$ molecule is

$$\begin{aligned} & (\sigma 1 \mathrm{~s})^2\left(\sigma^* 1 \mathrm{~s}\right)^2(\sigma 2 \mathrm{~s})^2\left(\sigma^* 2 \mathrm{~s}\right)^2\left(\sigma 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{z}}\right)^2\left(\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}\right)^2 \\ & \left(\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}\right)^2\left(\pi^* 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}\right)^1\left(\pi^* 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}\right)^1 \end{aligned}$$

Bond order of $\mathrm{O}_2$ molecule

$$=\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2}=\frac{10-6}{2}=2$$

content: Identify the hybridisation and geometry of $\mathrm{SF}_4$ molecule respectively.
explanation:
The molecule $\mathrm{SF}_4$ has sulfur ($\mathrm{S}$) as the central atom, which is bonded to four fluorine ($\mathrm{F}$) atoms. Sulfur has a total of six valence electrons, and with four of these used in bonding with the fluorine atoms, two electrons remain as a lone pair on the sulfur atom.

The hybridization of the central atom can be determined by counting the number of regions of electron density around it—these include both the bonded atoms and any lone pairs.

Hybridization: Sulfur in $\mathrm{SF}_4$ forms four sigma bonds with the fluorine atoms and has one lone pair. This sums up to five regions of electron density. The hybridization that accommodates five electron pairs is $\mathrm{sp}^3d$.

Geometry: The presence of one lone pair and four bonded pairs leads to a molecular geometry known as the "see-saw" shape. While the electron pair geometry of a molecule with $\mathrm{sp}^3d$ hybridization is trigonal bipyramidal, the actual molecular geometry (considering only bonding pairs) is "see-saw."

Therefore, the correct identification for hybridization and the associated electron pair geometry of $\mathrm{SF}_4$ is:

Hybridization: $\mathrm{sp}^3d$

Geometry: Trigonal bipyramidal (electron pair geometry)

The answer is Option A: $\mathrm{sp}^3d$ and Trigonal bipyramidal.

content: Which from following compounds is most covalent?
explanation:
The metal halides having metals in their higher oxidation states are more covalent than the ones having metals in lower oxidation state.

$$\begin{array}{llll} \mathrm{SbCl}_3, & \mathrm{PbCl}_2, & \mathrm{SnCl}_4 & \mathrm{SnCl}_2 \\ \uparrow & \uparrow & \uparrow & \uparrow \\ +3 & +2 & +4 & +2 \end{array}$$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{SnCl}_4$ is highly covalent in nature.

content: Which among the following forces of attraction is developed between polar and non polar molecules?
explanation:
Dipole-induced dipole interaction is the force of attraction developed between polar and non-polar molecules.

When a polar molecule, possessing a permanent dipole, approaches a non-polar molecule, its electric field can distort the electron cloud of the non-polar molecule, inducing a temporary dipole in it. This interaction results in an attractive force between the polar and non-polar molecules, stabilizing them.

This mechanism plays a significant role in many biological and chemical processes where polar and non-polar substances interact. It is generally weaker than permanent dipole-dipole interactions but stronger than inductions resulting from purely temporary dipoles alone.

In summary, the correct answer is:

Option A: Dipole-induced dipole interaction

content: What is the number of lone pair of electrons on central halogen atom in BrF$_3$?
explanation: Interhalogens of type $\mathrm{XX}_3^{\prime}$ have $\mathrm{sp}^3 \mathrm{d}$ hybridization. They have two lone pairs of electrons on central atom X.

content: Identify the bond order and magnetic nature of $\mathrm{Li}_2$ molecule respectively.
explanation:
To determine the bond order and magnetic nature of the $\mathrm{Li}_2$ molecule, we need to consider its molecular orbital configuration.

Electronic Configuration of $\mathrm{Li}_2$:

The atomic number of lithium (Li) is 3. In a $\mathrm{Li}_2$ molecule, each lithium atom contributes 3 electrons, totaling 6 electrons for the molecule.

The electrons fill molecular orbitals in the following order: $\sigma_{1s}$, $\sigma_{1s}^*$, $\sigma_{2s}$, $\sigma_{2s}^*$.

Filling the Molecular Orbitals:

$\sigma_{1s}$: 2 electrons (filled)

$\sigma_{1s}^*$: 2 electrons (filled)

$\sigma_{2s}$: 2 electrons (filled)

The $\sigma_{2s}^*$ orbital remains unfilled as we only need to allocate 6 electrons.

Bond Order Calculation:

The bond order is calculated using the formula:

$$ \text{Bond Order} = \frac{1}{2} \left( N_b - N_a \right) $$

where $ N_b $ is the number of electrons in bonding orbitals, and $ N_a $ is the number of electrons in antibonding orbitals.

For $\mathrm{Li}_2$:

Bonding electrons ($ N_b $): 4 (from $\sigma_{1s}$ and $\sigma_{2s}$)

Antibonding electrons ($ N_a $): 2 (from $\sigma_{1s}^*$)

Thus, the bond order is:

$$ \text{Bond Order} = \frac{1}{2} (4 - 2) = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 = 1 $$

Magnetic Nature:

Since all electrons are paired in the $\mathrm{Li}_2$ molecule, it is diamagnetic.

Conclusion:

Bond Order: 1

Magnetic Nature: Diamagnetic

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A: 1 and diamagnetic.

content: Identify the structure of $\mathrm{XeF}_4$ molecule from following.
Which from following molecules has two lone pair of electrons in valence shell of its central atom?
explanation:
The molecule $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ has two lone pairs of electrons in the valence shell of its central atom, oxygen.

In water ($\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$):

Oxygen is the central atom and is in Group 16 of the periodic table, meaning it has 6 valence electrons.

Each hydrogen atom forms a single bond with the oxygen atom, using 2 of the valence electrons (one for each $\mathrm{H-O}$ bond).

This leaves 4 electrons on the oxygen atom, which form 2 lone pairs.

In summary:

$\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ has 2 lone pairs and 2 bonding pairs on the central oxygen atom, leading to a bent molecular geometry due to the repulsion between the lone pairs and the bonding pairs.

Thus, Option C: $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ is the correct answer.

content: Which from following molecules is tetrahedral?
explanation: $\mathrm{CH}_4$ shows $\mathrm{sp}^3$ hybridisation, thus it has tetrahedral geometry.

content: What is the bond order in CO molecule?
explanation:
According to Lewis theory the bond order is equal to the number of bonds between the two atoms in a molecule.

$\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{O}$ has bond order 3.

content: What is formal charge on carbon in the following Lewis structure?
explanation:
Formal charge $$=\mathrm{VE}-\mathrm{NE}-(\mathrm{BE} / 2)$$

Formal charge on $$\mathrm{C}=4-0-(8 / 2)=0$$

content: Which activity from following is exhibited by Lewis base according to definition?
explanation:
According to the definition given by G. N. Lewis, a Lewis base is a substance that can donate a pair of electrons to a Lewis acid to form a Lewis adduct. A Lewis base, therefore, is an electron pair donor. So, the correct answer to this question would be:

Option B: donate a pair of electrons.

content: What is the geometry of $$\mathrm{PCl}_5$$ molecule as per VSEPR?

content: What is the bond order in N$$_2^+$$?
explanation:
Total number of electrons in $$\mathrm{N}_2^{+}$$ is 13.

So, its electronic configuration is

$$\sigma 1 s^2<\sigma^* 1 s^2<\sigma 2 s^2<\sigma^* 2 s^2<\pi 2 p_x^2=\pi 2 p_y^2<\sigma 2 p_z^1$$

So, $$\mathrm{BO}=\frac{N_b-N_a}{2}=\frac{9-4}{2}=2.5$$

content: Which of the following molecules does NOT obey octet rule?
explanation: In $$\mathrm{BeF}_2, \mathrm{Be}$$ has 4 electrons in its valence shell. Thus, it does not obey octet rule.

content: What is bond order of F$$_2$$ molecule?
explanation:
The electronic configuration of $$\mathrm{F}_2$$

$$\begin{aligned} & (\sigma 1 s)^2\left(\sigma^* 1 \mathrm{~s}\right)^2(\sigma 2 s)^2\left(\sigma^* 2 \mathrm{~s}\right)^2\left(\sigma 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{z}}\right)^2\left(\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}\right)^2 \left(\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}\right)^2\left(\pi^* 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}\right)^2\left(\pi^* 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}\right)^2 \\ & \text { Bond order of } \mathrm{F}_2 \text { molecule }=\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2}=\frac{10-8}{2}=1 \end{aligned}$$

content: Which metal halide from following has lowest ionic character ( $$\mathrm{M}=$$ metal atom)?
explanation: Ionic character of metal halides decreases in the order $$\mathrm{MF}>\mathrm{MCl}>\mathrm{MBr}>\mathrm{MI}$$, where, $$\mathrm{M}$$ is a monovalent metal. Smaller the size of anion, greater is the ionic character (Fajan's rule). Therefore, MI has the lowest ionic character.

content: Which among the following halogens combines readily with metals to form metal halides with highest ionic character?
explanation: Ionic character of metal halides decreases in the order $$\mathrm{MF} > \mathrm{MCl} > \mathrm{MBr} > \mathrm{MI}$$, where, $$\mathrm{M}$$ is a monovalent metal. Smaller the size of anion, greater is the ionic character (Fajan's rule). Therefore, fluorine forms metal halides with highest ionic character.

content: Identify a molecule with incomplete octet from following.
explanation: $$\mathrm{Li}$$ in $$\mathrm{LiCl}$$ has less than eight electrons in its valence shell. It has only four electrons. Hence, it has an incomplete octet. Molecules like $$\mathrm{SF}_6, \mathrm{PCl}_5, \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$$ have an expanded octet. They have more than eight electrons around the central atom.

content: Lewis acid is a substance that :
explanation:
Lewis acids are defined as substances that can accept an electron pair. According to the Lewis acid-base theory, an acid is an electron pair acceptor, and a base is an electron pair donor. This definition is broader than the Bronsted-Lowry definition of acids and bases, which involves proton transfer.

Given the options :

  • Option A : gives $\mathrm{H}^+$ ions in aqueous solution - This describes a Brønsted-Lowry acid, not necessarily a Lewis acid.

  • Option B : accepts a proton - This is the definition of a Brønsted-Lowry base.

  • Option C : accepts electron pair - This accurately describes a Lewis acid.

  • Option D : donates a proton - This is the definition of a Brønsted-Lowry acid.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C : accepts electron pair.

content: Which of the following is a Lewis acid but NOT a Bronsted acid?
explanation:
To identify which of the given compounds is a Lewis acid but not a Bronsted acid, we must first understand the definitions of Lewis and Bronsted acids.

A Lewis acid is a compound that can accept an electron pair, whereas a Bronsted acid is a compound that can donate a proton (H+). In other words, Lewis acids are electron pair acceptors, and Bronsted acids are proton donors.

Now let's evaluate the given options:

  • Option A: $$\mathrm{BCl}_3$$ - Boron trichloride ($$\mathrm{BCl}_3$$) is a Lewis acid because it has an incomplete octet; the boron atom has only six electrons in its valence shell and thus can accept an electron pair to complete its octet. However, it does not have a releasable proton, which means it cannot donate a proton in a reaction. Hence, $$\mathrm{BCl}_3$$ is a Lewis acid but not a Bronsted acid.

  • Option B: $$\mathrm{HNO}_3$$ - Nitric acid ($$\mathrm{HNO}_3$$) is both a Lewis acid and a Bronsted acid. It can donate a proton to become $$\mathrm{NO}_3^-$$ (making it a Bronsted acid), and it can also accept an electron pair because the nitrogen atom can participate in coordinate covalent bonding. However, since it is a Bronsted acid, it does not fit the requirement for this question.

  • Option C: $$\mathrm{NH}_3$$ - Ammonia ($$\mathrm{NH}_3$$) is a Lewis base because it has a lone pair of electrons that can be donated to form a bond. It is not a Lewis acid. Ammonia is also not a Bronsted acid, as it doesn't donate protons but rather accepts them.

  • Option D: $$\mathrm{HSO}_4^{-}$$ - Hydrogen sulfate ion ($$\mathrm{HSO}_4^{-}$$) is a Bronsted acid because it can donate a proton to become sulfate ion ($$\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$$). It does not readily act as a Lewis acid in accepting an electron pair, so its primary function is as a Bronsted acid.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option A: $$\mathrm{BCl}_3$$

content: What is the bond order of CO molecule?
explanation: Bond order of $$\mathrm{CO}=\frac{6-0}{2}=3$$

content: Which of the following is NOT hydrogen like species?
explanation:
Hydrogen-like species, also known as hydrogenic atoms or ions, are those that have only one electron surrounding the nucleus, irrespective of the charge on the nucleus. To evaluate which of the given species is not hydrogen-like, we need to look at the electron configuration of each.

Option A: $$\mathrm{He}$$ has two protons in its nucleus and two electrons. This is the normal helium atom and it has two electrons, hence it is not a hydrogen-like species since a hydrogen-like species can have only one electron.

Option B: $$\mathrm{He}^+$$ is a helium ion with one electron removed, thus it has two protons in its nucleus but only one electron. This species is hydrogen-like because it has only one electron.

Option C: $$\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$$ has three protons in its nucleus and, in this ionic state, has had two electrons removed, leaving it with just one electron. Like $$\mathrm{He}^+$$, this ion is hydrogen-like.

Option D: $$\mathrm{Be}^{3+}$$ has four protons in its nucleus and, as a trivalent cation, has had three electrons removed. Therefore, it has only one electron remaining, which makes it a hydrogen-like species.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A: $$\mathrm{He}$$, because it is not hydrogen-like due to its two electrons.

content: Identify the molecule in which central atom undergoes $$\mathrm{sp}^3$$ hybridisation?
explanation:
The central atom in a molecule undergoes $$\mathrm{sp}^3$$ hybridization when it forms four sigma ($\sigma$) bonds and has no lone pairs of electrons. Let's examine each option to identify the correct one:

Option A: $$\mathrm{BF}_3$$

The boron atom in boron trifluoride ($$\mathrm{BF}_3$$) has three bonding pairs and no lone pairs of electrons. As a result, boron is surrounded by three regions of electron density. Boron undergoes $$\mathrm{sp}^2$$ hybridization to form three $$\mathrm{sp}^2$$ hybrid orbitals, which overlap with the p orbitals of fluorine to form three $$\sigma$$ bonds. Hence, $$\mathrm{BF}_3$$ does not have $$\mathrm{sp}^3$$ hybridization.

Option B: $$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$$

In water ($$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$$), the central oxygen atom has two bonding pairs (with hydrogen atoms) and two lone pairs of electrons. The oxygen atom is thus surrounded by four regions of electron density, which necessitates the use of four orbitals (one s and three p orbitals) to hybridize into four equivalent $$\mathrm{sp}^3$$ hybrid orbitals. Two of these $$\mathrm{sp}^3$$ hybrid orbitals form $$\sigma$$ bonds with hydrogen atoms, and two accommodate the lone pairs. Therefore, water exhibits $$\mathrm{sp}^3$$ hybridization.

Option C: $$\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_4$$

In ethene ($$\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_4$$), each carbon atom forms three sigma ($$\sigma$$) bonds—two with hydrogen atoms and one with the other carbon atom. In addition, there is a pi ($$\pi$$) bond between the carbon atoms formed from unhybridized p orbitals. Each carbon atom in ethene undergoes $$\mathrm{sp}^2$$ hybridization to form three $$\mathrm{sp}^2$$ hybrid orbitals involved in sigma bonding, leaving one p orbital to form the pi bond. Thus, ethene does not exhibit $$\mathrm{sp}^3$$ hybridization either.

Option D: $$\mathrm{BeCl}_2$$

In beryllium chloride ($$\mathrm{BeCl}_2$$), the central beryllium atom forms two sigma ($$\sigma$$) bonds with two chlorine atoms and has no lone pairs. It uses its two available orbitals (one s and one p orbital) to hybridize into two equivalent $$\mathrm{sp}$$ hybrid orbitals. Therefore, beryllium in $$\mathrm{BeCl}_2$$ undergoes $$\mathrm{sp}$$ hybridization, not $$\mathrm{sp}^3$$.

Only Option B, $$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$$, has a central atom that undergoes $$\mathrm{sp}^3$$ hybridization. The correct answer is Option B: $$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$$.

content: What is the geometry of $$\mathrm{SbF}_5$$ molecule?
explanation:
The geometry of the $$\mathrm{SbF}_5$$ molecule can be determined using the VSEPR (Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion) theory. Antimony pentafluoride ($$\mathrm{SbF}_5$$) consists of a central antimony (Sb) atom surrounded by five fluorine (F) atoms.

According to VSEPR theory, the electron pairs around the central atom arrange themselves as far apart as possible to minimize repulsion. For a molecule with five bonding pairs and no lone pairs on the central atom, the geometry is trigonal bipyramidal.

In this structure, three fluorine atoms will occupy equatorial positions, forming a trigonal plane around the central antimony atom, and two fluorine atoms will occupy the axial positions above and below this plane, creating a linear line through the central atom.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option D
Trigonal bipyramidal

content: Identify the compound formed from elements X, Y, Z having oxidation state +2, +5, $$-$$2 respectively.
explanation:
To determine the correct compound formed from elements X, Y, and Z with oxidation states +2, +5, and -2 respectively, we need to balance the charges in the compound such that the total charge is zero.

Let's denote the oxidation states of X, Y, and Z as follows:

X: +2

Y: +5

Z: -2

Now we will analyze each option to see if the total charge equals zero.

Option A: $$\mathrm{X\left(Y_4 Z\right)}$$

In this case, the compound is $$\mathrm{X Y_4 Z}$$.

  • Oxidation state of X: +2
  • Oxidation state of Y: $$+5 \times 4 = +20$$
  • Oxidation state of Z: -2
  • Total charge: $$+2 + 20 - 2 = +20$$

This compound does not balance to zero, so it is not correct.

Option B: $$\mathrm{X_3 \left(Y Z_4 \right)_2}$$

In this case, the compound is $$\mathrm{X_3 \left(Y Z_4\right)_2}$$.

  • Oxidation state of 3X: $$+2 \times 3 = +6$$
  • Oxidation state of 2Y: $$+5 \times 2 = +10$$
  • Oxidation state of $$8Z: -2 \times 8 = -16$$
  • Total charge: $$+6 + 10 - 16 = 0$$

This compound balances to zero, so it is a possible correct answer.

Option C: $$\mathrm{X_3 \left(Y Z_2 \right)_2}$$

In this case, the compound is $$\mathrm{X_3 \left(Y Z_2 \right)_2}$$.

  • Oxidation state of 3X: $$+2 \times 3 = +6$$
  • Oxidation state of 2Y: $$+5 \times 2 = +10$$
  • Oxidation state of 4Z: $$-2 \times 4 = -8$$
  • Total charge: $$+6 + 10 - 8 = +8$$

This compound does not balance to zero, so it is not correct.

Option D: XYZ$$_2$$

In this case, the compound is XYZ$$_2$$.

  • Oxidation state of X: +2
  • Oxidation state of Y: +5
  • Oxidation state of 2Z: $$-2 \times 2 = -4$$
  • Total charge: $$+2 + 5 - 4 = +3$$

This compound does not balance to zero, so it is not correct.

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option B: $$\mathrm{X_3 \left(Y Z_4 \right)_2}$$

content: Which of the following concepts is NOT of valence bond theory?

options.content: Delocalisation of electron over the two nuclei
Combination of atomic orbitals to give molecular orbitals

explanation:
Valence bond theory focuses on the concepts of orbital overlap, hybridization, and the formation of covalent bonds. To determine which concept is NOT part of valence bond theory, let's briefly review each option:

Option A: Covalent character of bond

The covalent character of bonds is indeed a core concept of valence bond theory. It describes how atomic orbitals overlap to form bonds that share electrons between atoms.

Option B: Shielding effect of electrons

The shielding effect refers to the reduction of the effective nuclear charge on the electron cloud, due to a repulsion caused by electrons in inner shells. This concept is more related to atomic structure and periodic trends rather than the valence bond theory.

Option C: Delocalisation of electron over the two nuclei

Delocalization of electrons over two nuclei is a concept that can be included in the valence bond theory through the resonance concept. However, it is more comprehensively dealt with in molecular orbital theory.

Option D: Combination of atomic orbitals to give molecular orbitals

This concept is fundamental to molecular orbital (MO) theory, not valence bond theory. Molecular orbitals are formed by the linear combination of atomic orbitals and describe the behavior of electrons in a molecule more globally.

Therefore, the option that is NOT part of valence bond theory is:

Option D: Combination of atomic orbitals to give molecular orbitals

content: Which of the following is correct decreasing order of the repulsive interaction of electron pairs in a molecule?

options.content: bond pair - bond pair > lone pair - bond pair > lone pair - long pair
lone pair - bond pair > lone pair - lone pair > bond pair - bond pair
bond pair - bond pair = bond pair - long pair > lone pair - lone pair
lone pair - lone pair > lone pair - bond pair > bond pair - bond pair

explanation:
Correct decreasing order of the repulsive interaction of electron pairs in a molecule is, lone pair-lone pair > lone pair-bond pair > bond pair-bond pair

The bond pairs of electrons is shared by two atoms whereas the lone pair of electrons is only under the influence of central atom. So, the electron could of lone pair occupies more space as compared the bond pair. This causes greater repulsion between the lone pair-lone pair.

content: What is the bond order of B$$_2$$ molecule?
explanation:
The bond order of $$\mathrm{B}_2$$ molecule is 1. The ground state electron configuration of $$B$$ is $$1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^1$$. So, $$\mathrm{B}_2$$ molecule has a total of ten electrons, which are arranged in MOs.

$$(\sigma 1 s)^2\left(\sigma^{\circ} 1 s\right)^2\left(\sigma^2 s\right)^2\left(\sigma^{\circ} 2 s\right)^2\left(\pi_{2 p_y^1}=\pi_{2 p_y^1}\right)$$

Bond order $$=\frac{1}{2}$$ (Number of bonding electrons $$-$$ number of anti-bonding electrons) $$=\frac{1}{2}(6-4)=1$$

content: Which of the following molecule contain $$50 \%$$ p-character of hybrid orbital in C atom?

An sp-orbital is composed of one $$s$$-orbital and one p-orbital, and thus, it has $$50 \% s$$-character and $$50 \% p$$-character.

content: What type of inter molecular force is present between magnesium chloride and water?
explanation:
When magnesium chloride ($MgCl_2$) dissolves in water ($H_2O$), the primary type of intermolecular force involved is the ion-dipole interaction. Let's examine why this is the case and how it differentiates from the other options provided.

  • Ion-dipole interaction: This occurs between an ion and a polar molecule. Magnesium chloride dissociates into $Mg^{2+}$ and $Cl^{-}$ ions in water. Water is a polar molecule with a partial positive charge near the hydrogen atoms and a partial negative charge near the oxygen atom. The positive $Mg^{2+}$ ions attract the oxygen end of water molecules, while the negative $Cl^{-}$ ions attract the hydrogen end of water molecules, establishing ion-dipole interactions. This is the dominant interaction that facilitates the dissolution of $MgCl_2$ in water, making Option D (Ion-dipole interaction) the correct answer.
  • Dipole-induced dipole interaction: This occurs between a polar molecule with a permanent dipole and a nonpolar molecule or atom that does not have a permanent dipole. The electric field of the polar molecule induces a temporary dipole in the nonpolar molecule or atom. Given that $MgCl_2$ dissociates into ions, and water is polar, dipole-induced dipole interactions are not the primary interactions here.
  • Dipole-dipole interaction: This type of interaction happens between two polar molecules. Since $MgCl_2$ in water exists as ions rather than neutral polar molecules, dipole-dipole interactions are not the main interaction in this context.
  • Hydrogen bonding: Hydrogen bonding is a special case of dipole-dipole interaction that occurs when hydrogen is bound to a highly electronegative atom (F, O, or N) and is attracted to another electronegative atom. While water molecules indeed participate in hydrogen bonding among themselves, the primary interaction between water and dissolved $MgCl_2$ ions is ion-dipole, not hydrogen bonding.

Therefore, the correct answer to the question is Option D (Ion-dipole interaction).
